title: Buying the product
paperName: Paper 3 - Specialist Options: Approaches, Issues and Debates
summary: 9990 Consumer — point of purchase, impulse buying, and payment psychology.
learningObjectives: Analyse point-of-sale displays and checkout effects.
Evaluate pain of paying and cashless spending.

simpleExplanation.title: The moment of purchase
simpleExplanation.summary: Checkout zones trigger impulse buys; cards reduce the 'pain of paying'; satisfaction after purchase drives repeat custom.
steps: Pain of paying — cash hurts more than card.

flashcards.back: Unplanned, sudden, emotional, immediate — often low-cost items at checkout (POS).
Products placed at checkout queue — captures waiting time and last-chance impulse ( sweets, magazines).
Psychological discomfort when parting with money — stronger with cash than cards (Prelec & Loewenstein).
Contactless/card payment reduces pain of paying → higher spending and less budget awareness.
After expensive purchase, anxiety about correct choice — seek reassurance via reviews or returns policy (2.3.1).
Impulse buys skip information search/evaluation stages — low-involvement, heuristic-driven (2.3.2).

sections.content: **Buying the product** (2.4.3) completes 2.4. Contrast **impulse** vs **planned** with examples.

sections.question: A supermarket adds chocolate bars and phone chargers at checkout queues. Impulse sales rise 22%. After introducing contactless payment, average transaction value increases 15% but customers underestimate their monthly spend when surveyed. A buyer returns an expensive coat after sleepless nights worrying about the cost. Explain using buying psychology and evaluate the retailer's changes.
sections.solution:
**POS displays — impulse buying:** Checkout **waiting time** + **low-cost items** = classic **impulse purchase** trigger. Bypasses **decision stages** (2.3.1) — no search or evaluation. 22% rise confirms **POS effectiveness** (industry standard tactic).

**Contactless — pain of paying:** **Reduced physical money exchange** weakens **salience of spending** (Prelec & Loewenstein) → **15% higher transactions**. Shoppers **underestimate spend** — payment **decoupled** from consumption moment.

**Coat return — cognitive dissonance:** Expensive **high-involvement** purchase (2.3.1) → **post-purchase dissonance** (Festinger) — sleepless nights = psychological discomfort from perceived **bad decision** → **return** as dissonance reduction.

**Evaluate — retailer benefits:** Combined changes maximise **revenue per visit** — POS captures impulse; cashless removes spending friction.

**Evaluate — consumer welfare concerns:** **Exploits impulsivity** — checkout items target **children** and **fatigue** (end of shop). **Cashless overspending** — especially harmful for **budget-constrained** consumers. **Ethical tension** — profit vs **financial wellbeing**. **Returns policy** partially protects but dissonance causes **distress** before return.
